title_001: SQ Social IQ Test — Dopamine Lab
meta_001: SQ Social IQ Test — a 4-skill read on how you do people
meta_002: Reading the room · conversation · boundaries · making up. 16 questions, one map of your relationship skills.
html_006: The four skills you show between people,
html_009: Reading the room · keeping talk going · drawing lines to protect yourself · repairing what broke.
html_011: quiet people can score high on SQ too.
html_013:
※ A self-report test for reference only (not a clinical assessment).
    It measures your ‘relationship skills’ — not whether you’re an introvert or extrovert.
html_014: Your four relationship skills stack up in real time — the reveal comes at the end!
html_015: 👉 Answer as the real you — not the you you wish you were!
html_019: ※ SQ is a skill, not a personality — start with your lowest axis and relationships get easier.
html_020: 🧪 Dopamine Lab — more tests this way →
js_001: The vibe in the room suddenly goes cold — you?
js_002: Roughly see why it happened, and who set it off
js_003: Only notice it went cold way later
js_004: They seem like they want to wrap up the chat — you?
js_005: Catch the cue and wind it down smoothly
js_006: If it’s fun, you just keep going
js_007: Your message gets zero reaction in the group chat — you?
js_008: Read the timing and topic, adjust your tone
js_009: Don’t sweat it, carry on as usual
js_010: In a brand-new setting (a group, a new job), you?
js_011: Quickly read the unspoken rules and who’s who
js_012: Rules aside, you just go at your own pace
js_013: Stuck one-on-one with someone you barely know — you?
js_014: Break the silence with an easy question
js_016: In a conversation, you’re usually the one who?
js_017: Spends more time listening and asking
js_018: Spends more time telling your own stories
js_020: The reactions and mm-hmms come out on their own
js_021: Reactions feel awkward, so you listen quietly
js_022: The name and news of someone you met last time?
js_023: You remember, and ask about it first next time
js_025: Someone asks you for something unreasonable — you?
js_026: Can say no without damaging the relationship
js_027: Can’t say no, so you take it on anyway
js_028: Someone makes a joke that crosses a line — you?
js_029: Smile, but still make it clear — ‘that’s far enough’
js_030: Laugh it off and quietly get hurt
js_031: Your plans and hangouts pile up too high — you?
js_032: Dial back the count to protect your energy
js_033: Go to all of them, then crash at home
js_034: A relationship that only drains you — you?
js_036: Keep holding on, even when it hurts
js_037: After a fight with someone close — you?
js_038: Tend to be the one who reaches out first
js_039: Wait until they come to you
js_040: When you owe someone an apology — you?
js_043: You sense a misunderstanding brewing — you?
js_044: Don’t let it sit — you say ‘let’s clear this up’
js_045: Hope it just resolves on its own, unspoken
js_046: A dear connection you’ve drifted from — you?
js_047: Can send a ‘how are you’ first
js_048: Too awkward, so you never send it
js_050: Sensing the flow and signals in a room
js_051: Once a day, mid-conversation, guess: ‘How’s their mood right now, out of 10?’ Reading people grows with every rep.
js_053: Melting awkwardness and warming a bond
js_054: Keep three all-purpose questions in your pocket and the talk keeps flowing — ‘What are you into lately?’ ‘How’d you get into that?’ ‘So how did it turn out?’
js_057: Memorize one refusal formula — ‘I appreciate it (cushion), but I can’t this time (the no), though I could do this instead (alternative).’
js_060: The golden window for making up is 48 hours. One line — ‘I was on edge that day, sorry’ — is enough. The longer you write, the more it reads like an excuse.
js_062: All four skills near max, evenly. People gather around you for a reason.
js_064: Your relationship muscles are solid! Shore up just your lowest axis and any relationship gets easier.
js_066: The best partner for the right people, an energy drain in the wrong rooms. Maybe it’s not a skill issue — it’s an allocation issue.
js_068: Your own world comes before relationships. That’s not bad — it’s a style. Just try using the skills on the few who matter.
js_070: People just wear you out, don’t they. You don’t have to get along with everyone — start with one single connection.
js_073: ) is your most solid one — a skill you already use well in your relationships.

_app.title: SQ Social IQ Test
_app.desc: Not book-smarts, people-smarts — 16 questions on your relationship skills
